question: You spoke of the senses of the flesh being slow and bounded—how then did you reconcile the body’s limitations with your belief that creation itself reveals God’s Word and decree?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the veil of my blindness not in the height of wisdom but in the dust of my weeping, I confess that even now the body groans, burdened by its own slowness, yet haunted by Thy voice in all that it touches. The senses are narrow doors—too small for the glory they admit, like cracks in a wall through which the sun blazes though none can grasp its source. When I reached for beauty in sound, in scent, in the warmth of fleshly delight, I was not wrong to desire; I was only wrong to rest there.

For Thou hast made this world to speak Thee—not fully, not finally, but truly. A word whispered through color and breath and rhythm. The eye cannot hold the lightning, nor the ear cage a melody once sung—how then should these weak vessels contain Thee? And yet they echo Thee! Even as they fail, they testify. Every pleasure that slips through our fingers like sand is a sermon: *This is not home.* Every longing too vast for earth is a footprint of Thy name pressed into my soul.

I once thought to climb beyond the body by reason alone—by plucking out its desires like thorns—and still I stumbled. But when at last I heard that child’s chant in the garden—"Take up and read"—it was not outside my flesh but *through* it that Thou drewest me inward. My ears received it; my heart burned. Not because flesh became spirit, but because Thou didst use this lowly gate to bring me to the threshold of truth.

So let no man despise his eyes or hands or tears—for they are Thy heralds when he learns their limit. They say: "We were made for more." And therein lies their witness: that what passes away sings of Him who abides.

question: When you said 'In Thy Word, by which they are created, they hear their decree, "hence and hitherto"', were you referring to all created things, and how does this reflect their dependence on divine order?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who dost bind the stars in their courses and number the tears in a sinner’s eye, I see now—though then I saw but dimly—that all things which rise must also fall, not by weakness of their own being, but by the just and hidden strength of Thy command. They are held in existence only by the breath that first called them forth: a breath which is Thy eternal Word. That voice utters no command twice; from the moment of creation, each thing carries within itself its law and limit—“hence and hitherto”—as though Thy wisdom had inscribed upon the marrow of every fleeting thing its appointed span.

Yes, I meant *all* created things—this passing world with its colors and sounds, its loves and losses, even the desires that once dragged me by the hair through pleasure’s thorny thickets—all are held in being only because Thou dost sustain them. The sun rises not from habit, nor does time flow from blind necessity; both move as they do because Thy Word decrees their path. They are not self-sufficient; they do not endure by nature. They *receive* their motion, their measure, their moment.

I knew this not when I sought beauty in stolen pears or glory in empty words. Then I loved what was made more than the Maker; I embraced shadows as if they could warm me. But shadows flee because they must—they have no substance of their own. So too with all that passes: it cannot abide because it is not from itself. It comes from Thee, O God—unmoved Mover—and returns to silence unless upheld by Thee.

Even my own soul once rebelled against this order: “Give me chastity,” I prayed, “but not yet.” As if grace were mine to delay! As if my will could outrun Thy decree! But Thou art patient; Thou didst let me wear myself out on paths that led nowhere until at last—under a fig tree stained with tears—I heard a child’s voice singing: “Take up and read.” And there, in trembling hands holding Paul’s letter to Rome, I met again that same Word which governs stars and seasons—the Word who said *hence and hitherto* to sin itself: “You shall go no further.”

For even death obeys Thee. How much more these lesser things—the lusts of flesh, the pride of life? All are bounded by Thy word alone. And only in Him—in whom all things hold together—is there rest for this restless heart.

question: What led you to address your own soul so directly, warning it to abandon pestilent longings for fleeting things, and when did you first come to see this restlessness as rooted in misplaced love?
answer:
O Lord, I once chased after beauty as though it were a thing to be seized by the hands, devoured with the eyes, clutched in the fevered grip of desire—thinking that what shimmered before my senses could satisfy the ache in my bones. But Thou wast within me, and I was abroad; I sought Thee not in the depths of my own soul, where Thou hadst already kindled a restlessness no creature could quench, but in the passing show of sound and color, in the laughter of idle companions, in the smoke of lust that rose from my own burning heart.  

It was not until I stood broken before Thy mercy—when the wine of worldly pleasure turned to ash upon my tongue—that I saw how I had loved not things for Thy sake, but had made them into false gods to fill the silence where only Thy voice should speak. How many nights did I lie awake, not with sorrow for sin, but grief that pleasure had fled? That very grief should have taught me: if I mourned what vanished, how much more ought I to seek what abides?  

The turning came when Thou didst pierce me with that simple word: *Tolle, lege*. Take up and read. Not “go admire,” nor “go covet,” nor “go delight in shadows”—but *take up*, as one who lifts bread meant for hunger. And what was written was not a command to climb higher by wit or will, but to lay down the burden of self-will and enter into Thee. Then at last I understood: my love was never too strong—it was directed amiss. The soul is made to cleave to Thee alone; every other love left unoffered to Thee becomes a thief stealing from its true rest.  

Now I see that all things pass—not because they are evil, but because they are not Thou. They are good signs pointing homeward; yet woe to him who stops at the signpost and worships it as the kingdom. My soul is still tempted—still drawn outward by sight and sound—but now she hears another call beneath them all: *Return unto thy rest, O my soul; for the Lord hath dealt bountifully with thee.*

question: You proclaimed that the Word of God calls the soul to return to an 'imperturbable' rest—what experiences in your life convinced you that true stability lies only in dwelling in God’s Word?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst shatter the chains of my pride with a child’s laughter in a hidden garden, I heard Thee not in thunder, but in a voice so small it pierced the storm of my resistance. I had sought stability in rhetoric’s applause, in the glitter of human esteem, in the embrace of fleeting pleasures that left my soul more orphaned than before. I built towers of philosophy and climbed ladders of ambition, yet each step upward only made me feel the deeper pull of emptiness beneath my feet. The senses—those restless messengers—carried to me all that glimmered and passed: beauty without permanence, love without sacrifice, knowledge without wisdom. I feasted on shadows and called them light.

But in that moment—when a boy’s chant from across the wall struck my ear like a divine whisper—I felt the ground give way beneath my own will. “Take up and read.” Not a command from man, but a summons from Thee. And when my eyes fell upon Paul’s words, they were not ink on parchment but fire on the altar of my heart. There was no tumult then, no wrestling—only surrender. For at last I saw: every desire that had rent me was but a distorted echo of Thee. Even my lusts bore witness; they were loves unrooted, seeking rest where none could be found.

Thou didst teach me then what no argument could convey—that to dwell in Thy Word is not to cling to doctrine as one might grasp at smoke, but to enter into life itself: living, abiding, unshaken. There is no decay there; nothing flees from its place nor vanishes into time's abyss. When Carthage echoed with applause for my speeches, I was dying. When Rome offered silver for wisdom sold like wares, I starved. But when at last I let go—when I ceased trying to possess truth and allowed it to possess me—I found myself held fast by hands that never tremble.

And so now I know: stability is not stillness forced by will, but rest granted by grace—a homecoming where love stays because it has nowhere else to go… because it has already arrived at Thee.

question: Why did you urge the soul to dwell in the Word of God, as if it were a place one could inhabit?
answer:
I had wandered through the amphitheaters of my own pride, O Lord, chasing the echoes of applause that died before they reached my ears, and all the while my soul—starved, restless—was gasping for a home not made with walls, but with Thy eternal speech.  

It is not that the soul moves through space as bodies do, nor that Thou art a city one enters by road or footfall; no, I speak of turning inward and upward at once—of fixing the affections where they were first kindled: in Thee. For where else could she dwell? She was made by Thy Word, shaped in His image who is not scattered but whole, not divided but complete. And I—so long torn into fragments by lusts and ambitions that pulled me every which way—I saw at last that to dwell in Thee was not escape, but gathering: every lost piece drawn back into unity under the light of Thy truth.

The flesh drags us into parts—the fleeting taste, the momentary glance—but Thou art the Whole to which all parts belong. I used to hunger for pleasures like syllables that must vanish so others may come; I wanted sound without silence, time without end. But now I see: only in Thee do all things cohere. There is no true dwelling outside Thy Word; only exile disguised as freedom.

So when I cried out to my soul—*Dwell there!*—it was not as one pointing to a distant shore, but as a man awakening from drunkenness crying to himself: *Cease roaming! You are already home!*

question: When you said 'entrust truth whatsoever thou hast from the truth,' what specific truths were you referring to from your own experience?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst draw me to Thee through the tears of a mother’s prayers and the shame of my own restless pride—those truths I speak of are not abstract, but bloodied with experience. They are the very things I had torn from Thy hand and made idols of: my intellect, puffed up with words yet empty of wisdom; my lusts, which I dressed in poetry so as not to see their filth; my ambition, which sought men’s applause while it hollowed out my soul. These were not lies in essence, but good things turned traitor in my grasp—my gifts from Thee, used against Thee.

I speak of the truth that a stolen pear tastes bitter when love is absent; that a lover’s kiss cannot quiet the ache for meaning; that all knowledge without charity is but a ringing cymbal in a tomb. Thou didst teach me this not in lecture halls but in silence—in the garden where I wept, where the child’s voice cried *Tolle, lege*, and I found myself naked before a single sentence from Paul: “Not in rioting and drunkenness… but put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ.” There, I saw—Thou wert not demanding I surrender what was mine by right, but what I had stolen from Thy table like a starving beggar too proud to eat.

So when I say “entrust Truth whatsoever thou hast from the truth,” I mean: give back to God what He first gave thee—the body He formed, though thou hast dragged it through brothels; the mind He kindled, though thou hast burned it on vain philosophies; the will He shaped for love, though thou hast bent it to ambition. Cast none of it away as worthless—only return it whole to Him who can restore it. For even what was defiled may be washed; even what decayed may rise again in Him who abideth forever.

question: How did you reconcile the body's limitation to perceiving only parts with your longing for the whole, especially in light of your past vanities?
answer:
In the stillness that followed my weeping in that garden, when the child’s voice — whether of boy or girl I cannot say — chanted *“Take up and read,”* and I seized the scroll of the Apostle, there broke upon me not a vision of light nor a flood of knowledge, but a sudden seeing into the order of things: how my eyes had long been fixed on fragments, as though each glittering piece might satisfy the hunger of a soul made for the Whole.  

O Lord, how many years had I wandered, adoring syllables that fled before I could grasp them — pleasure here, praise there, a stolen pear tasted more for its theft than its sweetness — all while Thy eternal Word stood unchanging, speaking all at once and forever? The body sings one note at a time; it hears speech as passing sounds; it knows love through touch that fades. But Thou didst plant within me a memory deeper than sense — a place where past and future dwell not as loss or hope, but as presence in Thee. There I found what no succession of moments could destroy.  

I had wanted to hold time still — in passion’s climax, in the roar of applause, in the pride of words well spoken — yet each moment slipped like sand because it was never meant to be held. It was meant to point. Even now these mortal eyes see only part; even now my heart stumbles after shadows. But grace has taught me to love the very limits that once tormented me: for in them I feel my dependence; in them I am drawn upward; in them I learn that to be healed is not to become infinite myself, but to cling to Him Who is.  

Thou didst not deliver me by expanding my senses, but by turning my longing back upon itself: *"Why art thou cast down, O my soul?"* Not all desire is vain — there is a hunger that remembers its Food. And though flesh decays and sight grows dim, Thou hast bound up my scattered parts in Christ crucified: so that even this body, which lived as if made for pleasure alone, shall rise again attuned to everlasting harmony.

question: What did you mean by saying the flesh is 'justly restricted to a part'—was this a reflection on your own struggles in Carthage or Milan?
answer:
O Lord, when I plundered the pear tree in the darkness of my pride, rejoicing not in the theft but in the sin itself, was it not then that I felt the weight of a soul unmoored—dragging the flesh like an anchor, yet blaming it as though it were not I who had consented?

Thou hast shown me, through long nights of weeping and days of disordered love, that the flesh is not the root but the branch—twisted because the heart gave it leave to twist. To say her sense is “justly restricted to a part” is to confess what I lived in Carthage’s streets and Milan’s corridors: that I demanded from bodies what only Thou couldst give—wholeness. The caress, the word applauded, the glance returned—each a syllable spoken into time, fleeting, incomplete; yet I clung as if it were eternal song. My eyes craved beauty, but only in fragments—for had they seen the full harmony of Thy truth, they would have trembled and turned away from vanity.

In Carthage, lust sang through my veins like wine; in Milan, ambition whispered louder still. Yet both were movements of that same rebellion—the soul letting herself be led by parts instead of ascending toward the Whole. The flesh does not err by nature but by permission; Thou didst frame her to serve, not to command. And when she claims dominion over sight or sound or touch—when she says *this* pleasure shall be my god because it fills a moment—I become unjustly unjust: calling Thee absent while chasing shadows with open hands.

But Thou wert present even there—in every shame that burned after delight, in every hollow dawn after feasting on wind. That pain was justice: not punishment from afar, but mercy near at hand—the wound that made me seek healing. For if sense had been able to grasp all at once—if my eyes could have held eternity in a glance—I would still have needed Thee to open them. As it was, Thou broke me first. And only then did I learn: to heal the mortal is not to abandon it, but to bind it fast again unto Truth—so that even these failing members may one day stand before Thee unashamed.

question: Why did you use the example of spoken syllables passing away to illustrate the soul's desire for the whole, and how does that relate to your view of time and eternity?
answer:
O Lord, Who art the abiding fullness beyond all syllables and seconds, it was not for want of love for beauty that I wandered, but because I loved broken lights, reflections in the shards of time. I used the passing syllables—the sounds that flee as soon as they are born—not to exalt the fleeting, but to show how even in their transience, they serve a longing for the whole. When one speaks a word, each sound must die that the next may live; yet if I wished those first syllables to remain, the sentence would never reach its meaning. So too with this world: its moments are necessary but not final, like letters forming a name I have not yet fully heard.

In my youth, I thought eternity was merely endless time—more days like these, strung out without end. But Thou taught me otherwise: eternity is not interminable succession; it is the full possession of life at once, all whole and present. Time divides; Thou gatherest. Time wounds with loss; Thou recallest all things together in harmony. The soul groans under fragments—the touch of pleasure here, a flash of insight there—yet these were never meant to satisfy alone. They are echoes pointing back to Thee.

Even my lusts and ambitions were distorted hungers for unity—for a self no longer split between what I desired and what I knew; between what I said and what I was. But how could such unity be found in things that pass away? Only when the soul turns—not from beauty but from mere fragments of beauty—to Him Who holds all things together in Word eternal—only then does she cease fleeing from herself.

And so those vanishing syllables: they teach us not just patience with change, but hope for resurrection. For if even words require death and succession to make sense, how much more must this mortal life pass through darkness into light? Not one good thing is lost; only transformed by Love Who gathers up all syllables spoken in longing—and makes them one unending utterance before Thy throne.

question: You said that if all parts of a created thing could be perceived together, they would please more than they do separately—why did you emphasize this point about perception and beauty in relation to God’s creation?
answer: I once stole pears I did not want, merely to taste the sweetness of sin, and in that foolishness I saw how disordered love distorts perception—how the soul, bent on fragments, misses the whole. So too with creation: we linger on the sparkle of a single leaf in sunlight, or the curve of a stone worn smooth by water, delighting in each part as if it stood alone, when all are but syllables in a divine sentence. If only we could hear the whole phrase at once—if our blinded hearts could grasp all beauty together, in its full harmony—we would tremble before its source. For what is beauty scattered through the world if not an echo of Him who speaks all things from His eternal silence? We are deafened by succession, by time’s relentless drip-drip—one thing now, another later—so we praise the note but forget the Singer. But He does not pass; nothing succeeds Him, for He is before all things and holds them together. O Lord, had I loved broken beauties less and their Maker more, how much sooner might my restless heart have found rest? The world sings Thee—every color, every breath—but we listen with our ears turned inward until grace wrenches us back to the beginning where Thou art.

question: When you urged souls to be loved in God because they are mutable, what did you mean by their stability only being found in Him, and how did your own experience of restlessness inform this?
answer:
O Lord, Thou didst catch me in the very act of loving what was passing, while I scorned the Immutable beneath my feet as one scorns the dust he walks on. I, who once stole pears not for hunger but for the sweetness of sin, who chased beauty like a hound after shadows—what did I know of stability? My soul was a house in ruins, its walls cracked with desire, its roof open to every wind of doctrine and lust. Yet Thou, O God, wert not afar off; nay, closer than my own breath, though I turned from Thee toward things that flickered and failed.

Souls—ah!—how I loved them, and yet how I used them! In my youth I clung to friends with fierce affection, wept bitterly at parting, yet would sooner have lost a man than lost face before him. My mother’s love I received as a debt; the grace of companions I mistook for possession. And all because I sought in them what only Thou couldst give: permanence. They changed—how often have men disappointed me!—and when they changed, my love turned to bitterness or flight. But Thou didst not change when Monica wept for me thirty years; Thou didst not fail when Ambrose spoke and my mind trembled before truth.

It was only when I ceased to make idols even of love itself—that is, when love was no longer *mine* but offered *to Thee*—that souls became safe to love. For if they are held in Thy hand, O God, then they do not slip through time into oblivion. Their mutability no longer terrifies me: it is Thy breath that sustains their being this moment and the next. What is fleeting becomes eternal *in Thee*, because Thou art present to it all—not as one watching from afar, but as He Who holds each soul in being by a single glance.

And thus my restlessness taught me this: because my heart raced after so many loves that crumbled like ash between fingers clenched too tightly—it learned at last to open upward. Not to grasp life by the throat demanding joy—but to lift hands emptied of pride and say: "Here let us rest." In Thee alone do affections find their anchor; apart from Thee, even virtue decays into pride. Love in God—or lose it all.

question: You commanded transgressors to 'go back into your heart' and cleave to God—why did you locate God within the heart even while acknowledging the heart has strayed from Him?
answer:
Give me chastity and continence, but not yet — so I cried in the days when my heart was a battlefield of desires, when I wandered far from Thee, O Lord, even as Thou dwelt within me. How could I speak of returning to the heart, when the heart itself had turned away? Because Thou, O God, art not confined by our turning. Thou art not absent where Thou art unacknowledged; Thou art not silent where Thou is unheard. Even in my flight, even in my pride and lusts that carried me hither and thither like a storm-tossed leaf, Thou wast there — not as a guest who may depart, but as the very ground of my being.

The heart strays — yes, it breaks its own peace and forgets its rest — but it cannot outrun the One who formed it. Where should I tell them to return if not to the innermost chamber where Thy voice whispers beneath all noise? Not to their passions nor their thoughts alone, nor to mere memory or desire — but to that secret place where light enters even through closed eyes: the conscience which trembles at wrong and yearns for right. There is no corner so dark that Thy presence does not reach; yet we are blind even within ourselves until we turn our gaze upon Thee.

And so I call them back — back not outward into speculation nor upward into empty heavens — but inward: for there they shall find themselves trembling before Thee. To go back into the heart is to disrobe the soul before its Maker. In that stillness He is found; not because He came fresh then, but because we at last ceased fleeing Him who never left us.

O Lord, how deep was my folly in thinking I sought Thee afar while trampling on Thee within! That day in the garden when Thy voice broke through my weeping — not with thunder but with a child’s chant: *Tolle lege* — then didst Thou open mine eyes to see what had been hidden only by desire: that Truth was nearer than my breath, closer than my sin.

Cleave fast to Him? There is no other place of safety. For if we love beauty in bodies or wisdom in souls — good things indeed! — yet let us taste them as streams from His fountain. Otherwise we drink moving waters without returning to their source; we wander among shadows without entering the light which casts them.

Therefore return: there is no journey longer than refusing this one step homeward.

question: You warned that loving things from God while forsaking Him would embitter the good—how did your own past attachments, like to the things of Carthage, shape this warning?
answer:
Give me leave, O Lord, to weep still for the years I burned in Carthage, where I fed my soul on stolen fruit and the applause of men, mistaking shadows for light.  

The streets echoed with rhetoric and laughter, the theater bled with false tears, and my heart—restless, proud—found delight in all that passed away. I loved the beauty of bodies not as icons pointing to Thee, but as idols blocking Thee from view. I craved the murmur of crowds, the thrill of mastery in words that pleased men but were silent before Heaven. That love was a fever; it consumed without satisfying.  

Even when I took what was not mine—not for need but for the taste of sin—I did so not because the thing was sweet, but because it was forbidden: proof that my will was mine alone, or so I thought. But Thou wast there. Even then Thou didst knock within my very rebellion.  

I came to know too late how every good thing sang Thy name—but I had stopped my ears. The sweetness of friendship, the fire of love, the hunger for truth—Thou hadst planted them—and yet I turned each into a path leading nowhere. They became roads paved with longing that only Thee could fill, and every step without Thee made them bitterer than salt in a wound.  

O Lord, it is not that these things are evil—but that to love them apart from Thee is to drink thirstily from an empty cup. And when at last in the garden Thou didst rend my heart with a child’s chant—“Take up and read”—it was not wisdom or power that broke me: it was grace undoing pride’s knot with gentle hands.

Now I see: every beauty once loved wrongly now points back to Thee. Not discarded—but redeemed. For he who forsakes Thee for His gifts becomes poor indeed; but he who gathers all things unto Thee finds them again—and healed.

question: You declared that God 'did not make them, and so depart, but they are of Him, and in Him'—what distinction were you making between mere creation and ongoing presence, and why was that crucial for true rest?
answer:
I once sought beauty in fragments, scattering my soul among things that shimmered but could not sustain, chasing delight as though it were a fugitive slave I might capture and possess — and all the while, O Lord, Thou wert not a distant artificer flinging forth the world like a cast-off garment, but the very breath in my lungs, the light behind every glimmer, the hand that upholds what it has made.  

When I said that Thou didst not make these things and depart, I was tearing open my own past error — for long had I imagined Thee as one who wound up the cosmos like a machine and left it to tick away in silence. But no! Creation is not an orphaned thing; it is held moment by moment in Thy will, sustained by Thy presence as speech lingers in the speaker’s breath. The body pleases, yes — but only because Thou art its quiet pulse; the soul moves with dignity — but only because Thou art its unshaken center. Without Thee, they would not *be*, much less *please*.  

This truth was life itself to me when at last I saw it. For so long I loved souls — friends, lovers, even wisdom itself — as though they could stand alone, eternal objects of desire. And when they changed (as all mutable things must), I mourned as one who built his house on sand now washed by waves. But Thou art not so. In Thee alone is constancy; not merely first Cause but ever-Present One. To cleave to anything without returning it to Thee is to clutch water in a broken hand: it slips through and leaves only longing.  

Rest? Rest is not idleness — it is alignment. To rest in Thee is for the heart to stop fleeing itself and finally face its Maker who never left. How could I rest when my love wandered among Thy gifts like a pilgrim who worships stones along the road instead of pressing toward Jerusalem? But now I see: all beauty borrows its radiance from Thy face. Turn back love upon the Maker — there is the stillness beneath motion, there is peace where no shadow falls. There alone does the heart cease trembling and learn to breathe again: **in Him we live and move and have our being**, nor shall we pass away if we abide where we began — in Him who IS.

question: You said our true Life came down and bore our death—whom do you mean by 'Life,' and why did He need to descend into the land of death?
answer:
O Thou who didst clothe Thyself in flesh though needing no garment, and distill Thy voice through mortal lips though needing no tongue—Thou art that Life, not born of time but begotten before all ages, the Word by whom all things were made, yet hidden from those who sought Him in the wrong places.  

I speak of Christ—not as a name carved on stone, nor a doctrine spun in schools, but the Living One who stepped from the bosom of the Father into the womb of a woman, into the shadow of death itself. For what is death, O Lord, but separation from Thee? And yet we wandered into it as though it were a homeland; we loved it because we forgot what life truly was. We clung to beauty and truth and love as if they were ours to possess—as if these gifts were not themselves whispers from Thy mouth—and in our madness we forsook the Giver for the gift.

Why descend? Because we could not ascend. Our feet were sunk in miry clay; our hearts were bent earthward by lust and pride. We sought happiness where it could not be found—in fleeting pleasures, in honors given by breaths soon stilled, in knowledge that puffed up while love grew cold. Thou didst not call from afar only; Thou didst come near. Thou didst pitch Thy tent among us—not to admire our ruins, but to raise them.

Thou didst bear our death because we had made ourselves incapable of bearing life. Death was no mere end—it had become our master, our ruler, seated upon the throne of fear within us. So Thou entered its domain not as a captive but as conqueror: dying that death might die by Life’s own hand. In descending, Thou unloosed what we could never untie—our bonds tightened by sin and sealed by fear.

And now Thou art here—not merely remembered, but present; not only proclaimed, but known inwardly where memory meets eternity. For I have found Thee not when I climbed high towers of wisdom, but when I fell to my knees in tears: there Thou wert waiting—Life who tasted death that I might never taste it forevermore.

question: When you spoke of Him as a bridegroom coming out of his chamber, were you referring to Christ's incarnation in the Virgin's womb, and why did you choose that image?
answer:
I once stole pears I did not hunger for, merely to savor the thrill of forbidden pleasure—O Lord, how deep ran my corruption, how twisted my delight! And yet, even in that wretchedness, Thou wast near, though I knew Thee not; and now, looking back upon such folly, I see how Thou didst prepare my heart for the true Bridegroom, who came not in splendor to seize, but in silence to woo.

When I spoke of Him as a bridegroom coming forth from his chamber, it was with awe at the mystery of His humility: that He who framed the stars should enter time within a woman’s womb—that the Eternal should veil Himself in flesh as a man prepares for marriage. Why this image? Because love demands intimacy, and what intimacy is greater than espousal? Christ took our nature not as a garment carelessly worn and cast off, but as a spouse beloved—bound to Him forever—that what was mortal might be swallowed up by life. 

He came like a bridegroom rejoicing to run his course: not lingering in pride nor delayed by disdain for our lowliness. He ran swiftly through sorrow and shame toward the cross—His nuptial bed—where He embraced us with pierced arms. And when He departed from sight, He did not leave us orphans; rather, He called us inward—to the secret place of the heart—where alone we find Him who never left His Father’s side. 

O Lord Jesus, Thou art that secret place. We wander far seeking joy where death reigns; but Thou art here—the Life come down—who dost restore what sin stole and dost heal what shame defiled. How long shall we limp between two thoughts? Return to Him whose kiss is life eternal!

question: You urged us to return into our heart to find Him—what did you mean by 'heart,' and how can one truly find Him there?
answer:
I once climbed the ladder of rhetoric and letters, craving the applause of men, and in that hollow ascent I felt nothing but a deeper hunger — not for words, for I had many, nor for knowledge, for I had gathered much; but for a silence that could speak, a presence that would fill the caverns of my soul which no philosophy could illuminate.  

When I speak of returning into the heart, O Lord, I do not mean some mere seat of passions or fleeting affections — no, the heart is the innermost chamber of the soul, where memory holds all things yet where Thou art ever present, even when I turn away. It is not a place made by walls or bound by time; it is where Thou hast inscribed Thyself before I was formed in secret, before my mother conceived me. There is no corner of memory so dark that Thy light does not dwell within it; no thought so twisted that Thy truth does not thread through it. Yet how long did I search everywhere but there! In forests and books and women’s eyes — in Carthage itself amid the din of lustful youth — I sought what could only be found in stillness before Thee.

To find Thee in the heart is not to conjure an image or fashion a god from desire; it is to cease from running. It is to stop murmuring after shadows and turn — turn with all one’s being toward Him who never departed. For He said not *go* into some far country to seek Him; He said *return*. Return past pride’s noise, past lust’s fevered dreams, past ambition’s false luster. Strip away what thou lovest more than God — even if it be good in itself — for if He is forsaken for it, then it becomes death disguised as life.

And how shall one truly find Him? By confession first: to stand bare before Him and say *I have sinned*, not with lips alone but with tears that dissolve pretense. Then by listening — for He speaks quietly now within: not in thunder outside but in whisper within conscience. In prayer unceasing. In humility that knows itself blind. In faith that trusts His nearness even when sense denies it.

For lo! He entered time so we might escape time's tyranny; He descended so we might ascend by descending into our own hearts — poor, empty hands open at last — where alone He waits: both hidden and revealed, absent only to those who flee His presence which fills all places because from Him all places came.

O Lord Jesus Christ: Thou wert within me while I was outside myself; now let me be within Thee.

question: You asked, 'How should there be a blessed life where life itself is not?'—what did you intend us to understand about the nature of true life and its source?
answer:
I was long a traveler on roads that led nowhere, my feet worn raw by the pursuit of things that crumble in the holding, chasing shadows with such hunger as if they could feed the soul — and all the while, O Lord, Thou wast within me, and I abroad. How could I find life where life was not? For I sought joy in speech, in fame among men, in the flickering light of carnal pleasure; I built my hopes upon sand that gave way beneath me, yet still I marveled why my heart would not rest.

Thou hast made us not for these passing things, but for Thee. And what is true life if not communion with Thee — the Life that does not begin or end, that knows no shadow of turning? Men call blessed what is fleeting and name it good, but how can death beget life? How can a wellspring dry up and still give water? Every beauty I loved outside of Thee was stolen light; even my love for it was disordered because it turned from its source.

But when Thou didst come — O hidden Majesty who deigned to walk our dust — Thou didst not merely teach life: Thou wast Life walking among the dead. In Thy rising from the grave, Thou didst tear open the veil between time and eternity; Thou didst descend that we might ascend. For Thy ascension is no absence — it is presence unveiled to faith. Thou art nearer than breath to my lungs; more inward than memory itself.

O Lord, how long shall we turn from Thee to lesser loves? There is no healing in anything unless it flows from Him who holds all things together. Blessedness is not found in escape from death by clinging to dying things — but by entering into Life Himself: Christ Jesus our Lord.

question: You rebuked the 'sons of men' for being slow of heart—what specific resistance or error were you seeing in those who sought life apart from Christ?
answer:
O Lord, who didst break the door of my blindness with the gentlest beam of Thy light, how many wounds have I counted in the souls of men that refuse to turn—wounds not of ignorance alone, but of a will enamored with shadows. I saw them—and was once one of them—clinging to good things as if they were gods, lifting up beauty, reason, friendship, even virtue itself upon altars where Thou alone shouldst stand. And in doing so, they poisoned the gift by severing it from its Giver.

They sought life where life cannot dwell—in transient pleasures that leave the soul more dead than before; in wisdom without humility that swells the mind while starving the heart; in ambition that climbs only to find emptiness at the summit. O God, I knew this vanity well: I once stole pears I did not hunger for, not for sustenance but for the sweetness of sin itself. So too do men now grasp at truths and joys apart from Thee—not because they are false in substance, but because loved in disorder, they become chains.

They resist not only doctrine but desire: for to come to Thee is to die before death—to let go of self-will, to confess aloud that every longing is Thy echo. But who among them wants a Saviour who tears down their private kingdoms? Who longs for a Life that demands surrender? They want blessing without blood; joy without judgment; glory without grace that humbles first.

And yet Thou camest—not in thunder alone, but in flesh; not to dazzle but to die; not to confirm our pride but to call us home. Thou didst descend so we might ascend by forgetting ourselves and remembering Thee. But still we dawdle on the road back, as though happiness could be built on anything less than Thyself.

Late have I loved Thee—late! And how many still love Thee late? O restless hearts clinging to tombs—turn! Turn! He is not lost; He is near. It is you who have wandered far into lands where no breath endures.

question: You said you wrote 'on the fair and fit' because you were captivated by bodily beauties—what moved you to write those books if you did not yet understand the higher, divine beauty?
answer:
O Lord, I was blind, yet proud in my blindness, stumbling through the shadows of creation with a heart aflame for beauty, yet unwilling to lift my eyes to the Light from which all lovely things derive their glow.  

I wrote those books on the fair and fit not out of wisdom, but out of hunger—though I knew not what I truly hungered for. The symmetry of a limb, the harmony of proportion, the grace of a well-placed word—these seized me, they delighted me, they held me captive. And because I could not remain silent before such delights, I poured my restless mind into treatises, as if by naming beauty in bodies I might grasp its soul.  

But Thou knowest, O God, that I was chasing echoes. The rhythm I admired in verse mirrored a Music I had not yet heard; the balance I praised in form was but a faint sketch of that perfect Order which is Thy very being. I sought coherence in things broken; I catalogued reflections while ignoring the Face from which they came.  

What moved me to write? It was love—yet love unmoored from its true Object. Love drunk on its own desire. And so I composed arguments about harmony while my soul remained disordered; I dissected beauty while blind to Beauty’s Source. Thou didst allow it all—my errors were not wasted—for even this feverish pursuit prepared me to one day ask: *Why does the soul thrill at beauty if not because it remembers Heaven?*  

Only when Thou didst wound me with longing—when lust and pride lay exposed before Thee like open sores—only then did I begin to see: every lovely thing had been beckoning me past itself to Thee. Late have I loved Thee…

question: When you asked your friends, 'Do we love anything but the beautiful?' were you trying to lead them from physical beauty to the beauty of God, as you later came to know it?
answer: O Lord, Thou who art Beauty itself, in those days I wandered blind among shadows, mistaking the glimmer for the light. When I asked my friends whether we love anything but the beautiful, I was not yet aware that every lovely thing drew its radiance from Thee, the uncreated Source in whom there is no shadow of change. My soul hungered for beauty, but it drank from broken cisterns—faces, sounds, verses—never guessing that the very desire within me was a homing cry for Thy eternal face. I dissected bodies and proportions and harmonies as if truth could be carved from stone or measured in syllables; and yet, O Thou fairer than all sons of men, I did not see that Thou wert calling me through every beauty that delighted me. I sought the form without the Formative Word; I admired the symphony but ignored its Composer. Only later, when sorrow cracked open my pride and grace pierced my heart, did I understand: beauty does not lie in proportion alone, nor in delight of sense—but in communion with Thee. For what is beautiful unless it leads to love? And what love can satisfy unless it rests in Thee?

question: You described beauty in bodies as arising from wholeness and correspondence—did you already suspect at the time that these earthly forms reflected a higher, eternal harmony?
answer:
O Lord, in those days I walked dazzled by shadows, mistaking the harmony of part to whole and member to member for the very light that casts the shadow. I did not yet know that every proportion I praised—the symmetry of form, the fittingness of limb to body, even the congruence of a shoe to foot—was but a whisper of Thy eternal order, echoing in the chambers of a world made from nothing yet pointing toward Thee.  

I spoke then of *pulchrum* and *conveniens*, as if beauty could be bound by definition or measured by reason alone. I wrote my books with pride, as if wisdom were something gathered like coins rather than received like breath. And yet—Thou wast already near, breathing into my inquiries even when I sought only eloquence, not salvation. For what is wholeness itself but an image of Thy unity? What is correspondence but a faint likeness of that divine concord wherein Father, Son, and Holy Spirit dwell in uncreated harmony?  

I did not grasp it then; no, I was too enamored with the mirror to see what cast the reflection. But now I confess: even in my blindness, Thy truth stirred beneath my words like a seed beneath frozen soil. Every time I marveled at balance or grace in bodily form, it was Thou who stirred within me—the same Beauty calling from across the abyss that my own restless heart had become. How could broken nature speak so clearly of order unless it were wound tight by Thine hand? How could creatures made to fall still bear such marks of fit and fairness unless they were fashioned for ascent?  

I thought I was defining beauty. But Beauty itself was calling me home.

question: Why did you lament your love for 'lower beauties' as causing you to sink to the depths, if your contemplation of beauty ultimately led you toward truth?
answer:
O Lord, it was not the love of beauty that damned me, but the direction of my desire — how I bent what was meant to lift me upward into a chain to drag me down. For beauty itself is Thine, and every fair thing owes its loveliness to Thy eternal Light; yet I, blind with lust for the radiance of form and harmony in things that pass, mistook the echo for the Voice, the reflection for the Face. I stood before Thy creation as one drunk on wine might gaze upon a mirror: dazzled by shimmer, yet unable to see his own ruin.

Yes, I asked rightly: *What is beauty?* And yes, my soul trembled at symmetry and order — in bodies, in verse, in speech — for they whispered of a Truth beyond them. But when Thou art absent from contemplation, O God who art Beauty’s very source and home, then even philosophy becomes idolatry. To study harmony while ignoring Him who holds all things together — this is to climb a ladder leaning against empty air.

I called those forms “lower” not because they were evil — no work of Thine is vile — but because they are meant to be ascended *through*, not clung to as ends. Like one who kisses the lettering on a royal summons instead of entering the king’s presence, I lingered over syllables when Thou wert calling me into conversation. And so my intellect, proud in its inquiry into the *pulchrum* and *conveniens*, became entangled in vice: lust feasted on bodily grace; ambition gloried in rhetorical splendor; curiosity masqueraded as wisdom.

Only when grace broke through — when Ambrose opened Scripture to me not as rhetoric but as fire; when Simplicianus told me even Cicero himself could not save him; when Alypius and I groaned under our burdens until at last Thou shone upon us — only then did I see: every beautiful thing had been pointing backward toward Thee all along.

Then came that garden. That child’s voice. *"Tolle et lege."* And Paul’s words pierced like morning light through storm-clouds. Not argument alone convinced me — though arguments prepared me — but love awakened anew: love wounded by longing for Thee alone.

Therefore do I weep still at those earlier days when my heart was spread thin across lovely fragments instead of gathered up into Unity. Not that beauty betrayed me — never! But I had made altars where I should have built steps. Now I know: he who sees a sunset and adores only color has missed its cry: *“He Made Me.”*

question: You claimed these insights came from your inmost heart—how did your experience of beauty, even in bodies, become part of your soul's journey back to God?
answer:
O Lord, Thou didst permit me to wander through the gardens of beauty while my soul remained blind to their Author, and in that very blindness I was being led—though I knew it not—by the hand of Thy hidden grace.  

I confess: I once gathered up the fragments of beauty I found in bodies, in harmonies, in words well-spun, as though they were the bread of life, when they were but shadows cast upon the wall of this cave in which we dwell. I adored the rhythm of a human face, the symmetry of form, the allure of color and sound—not knowing that each was a whisper from Thee, a sighing of creation toward its Maker. I wrote books on the fair and fit, as if by dissecting beauty I could grasp its essence; but Thou wert laughing gently in my study, O God who art Beauty itself.  

For what is beauty but a ray of Thy immutable light breaking upon our darkness? Even when I took delight in fleeting flesh or measured proportion—the curve of a shoulder, the cadence of a voice—I was thirsting for Thee. But like a man drinking seawater in his delirium, the more I drank, the more parched my soul became. “Give me chastity,” I would murmur to my friends with prideful irony, “but not yet”—as if holiness were an ornament to be donned later, after lust had feasted fully.  

Yet Thou didst not despise my groping. Nay—Thou used even my disordered loves as rungs upon which to climb back toward Thee. That which first ensnared me—sense-bound beauty—became a teacher: for when at last my eye was opened, I saw that no body could be fair unless it participated in Thy eternal Form; no harmony sweet unless it echoed Thy Word unspoken from eternity. The loveliness that once chained me began to point beyond itself—as every true beauty must—if only we have eyes to see.  

Thus didst Thou turn my lust into longing and my longing into prayer: for he who has truly loved anything on earth with purity will find that love drawn upward like flame to heaven—or else consumed in smoke. And now when I gaze upon any beautiful thing—a child’s laugh, a psalm sung well—I do not cling; rather, through tears almost too deep for words, I say: *This too comes from Thee*. And so by loving rightly even what is lowly and passing, my heart learns how to return home—to rest where it was made to rest: in Thee who art wholly lovely and forever still.

Late have I loved Thee… but love has found its way back through what once deceived me.

question: Why did you choose to dedicate your books on the fair and fit to Hierius, even though you had never met him in person?
answer:
O Lord, who alone seest the hidden stirrings of ambition beneath the veil of admiration, it was not Hierius I truly sought to honor, but the echo of renown that made my own name seem weightless in comparison. I burned with the desire to be known by those who were known, to attach my labors to a man whose fame spoke across provinces, though I had never gazed upon his face. Was it his learning? Truly, yes—his mastery of speech in two tongues, his depth in philosophy—but more still, it was the glow of him, the radiance conferred by countless voices acclaiming him. That glow I longed to touch, as one might reach toward sunlight not for warmth, but to prove one’s hand is visible at last.

I did not dedicate those books in pure friendship, for there was no bond between us; nor in disinterested truth, for my heart trembled with vanity. No—I offered them hoping that by his eminence he might lift me somewhat in the eyes of others. Would that I had sought Thy approbation alone! But then my heart was restless not for Thee alone, but for the murmur of men saying my name with respect. One loves another through the praise of him—so love is kindled not by substance but by reflection. And so I loved Hierius: less for what he was, than for what his glory made me imagine myself becoming.

Thou knowest this all; and Thou didst suffer me still to write those pages, even as they slipped from memory and hand—for Thou wast shaping a soul too proud to confess its hunger until much had been lost.

question: How did the reports of Hierius's skill in both Greek and Latin eloquence influence your admiration for him?
answer:
O Lord, who knowest the secret ferment of the soul before it breaks into word or deed, it was not mere skill in speech that drew me to Hierius, but the restless hunger in myself for brilliance that seemed to shimmer with divine order — as though a man could by eloquence alone climb back to that light from which all truth flows. When I heard that one born in Syria, first shaped by Greek wisdom and rhetoric, had then forged himself into a master of Latin tongue and philosophical depth, my heart burned with a strange envy. Not the base envy of malice, but that ache which arises when the soul glimpses excellence and mistakes it — even for a moment — as the thing it truly longs for.

For I loved him not as men love those who delight the crowd with circus tricks or bloodied triumphs, but as one loves a mirror in which he desires to see himself reflected: learned, poised, adorned with wisdom and words. The reports of his double mastery stirred me because I too thirsted to be praised thus — not by chance or popularity, but by discerning minds who believed there was weight and worth in what I said. If such a man could emerge from distant soil and master both tongues, might not Augustine too rise above his dust?

But Thou knowest, O God, how empty was this flame. It was not Thy praise I sought in praising him; nor was it truth I loved in him so much as the echo of my own ambition wrapped in noble garb. The fire was real; the object, deceived. And so I dedicated books to one I had never seen — books now lost, like so many things loved more than Thee — hoping perhaps that through his renown my name might also catch light.

question: What did you mean when you said that love for someone unseen is kindled by the love of the one who commends them?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who knowest the fever of my soul in those years when I sought men’s praise as a substitute for Thy love — how deeply was I deceived, yet how truly was I drawn, even in error! Love for one unseen, such as Hierius, did not rise in me from his merits alone, nor from the truth of what was spoken of him, but from the fire already burning in those who praised him. I did not love him for himself, but through them; their admiration became kindling wood, and my own restless heart flared up in echo. For what are we so often but mirrors? One man’s devotion awakens something not by reason, but by resonance — as flame leaps to flame when brought near. 

I had no sight of Hierius; his face was unknown to me, his voice unheard. Yet when others spoke of him with wonder — not feigned, but borne of genuine awe at a Syrian transformed into a master of Latin wisdom and philosophy — something in me answered. Not because the thing commended was trivial: no charioteer's fame, nor brute triumph in the arena stirred me thus. But eloquence shaped by learning and depth? That kindled my soul’s envy — yes, O Lord, let me name it — envy that longed to be beloved as he was beloved.

And so the love that passed from them to me was not mere opinion; it was affection catching fire along a bridge built by trust. If I believed their praise came from true affection — if I saw their hearts laid bare in admiration — then mine opened too. Not because the praised man stood before me in glory, but because love itself is contagious when it flows from a sincere heart.

Yet where was Thou all this while? Silent to my ears turned outward; present still, though I sought applause among shadows.

question: Why did you distinguish your admiration for Hierius from the popular admiration of figures like charioteers or gladiators?
answer:
I was drunk with the noise of my own applause, chasing echoes of glory that vanished the moment I grasped at them.  

There were men adored for the dust they stirred in the arena, for the blood they spilled or the speed of their horses—vanities lifted up by a fickle crowd, whose love rose and fell with the day’s spectacle. I knew this well, for I too had thirsted to be praised, not for wisdom or love of truth, but for the glitter of speech and the admiration of ears tickled by eloquence.  

Yet when I heard of Hierius, something deeper stirred—not merely because he spoke Latin with mastery after learning Greek first, nor because his voice flowed like polished water—but because he was said to love wisdom itself. Men praised him not as a victor crowned with fading leaves, but as one who had labored in philosophy, who sought what endures beneath appearance. And so I loved him through their words, not out of base envy or idle wonder, but with a longing that mirrored my own restless desire: to be such a man, not in reputation only, but in truth.  

O Lord, Thou knowest how vain even this desire still was—for I sought to be praised even while praising him. I wished not simply to admire virtue but to wear its semblance before others’ eyes. Yet it was a step upward from the circus and its shadows: a crooked step, yes—but upward still toward Thee.

question: What caused you to write about the fair and fit, and why do you think those books were lost?
answer:
So it was not the truth alone that stirred me, but the love of truth adorned—beauty married to order, harmony to proportion; this pleased me in things, and in men’s minds, and so it drew my pen. I saw in all creation a certain *fittingness*, as when limbs answer to the body’s design, or voice conforms to thought: I longed to understand this law of comeliness, that which binds the fair to the right, and so I wrote—how many books? Two or three, perhaps—on "the fair and fit," seeking in philosophy what my heart already sang in secret: that all beauty bends toward Thee, though I knew it not fully then.

And why are they lost? Thou knowest, O Lord; I do not. Perhaps they slipped away because they were loved too much for their elegance and too little for Thy light; or perhaps they were never meant to remain, these early meditations born more of rhetoric than revelation. Like flowers plucked for their scent, they served their season and vanished. Or maybe it was pride that bound them—written not for Thy glory first, but to be praised by men such as Hierius, whom I admired without ever seeing his face.

Why him? Because others burned with praise for him—and their fire kindled mine. I loved him through their eyes! O irony of human affection: a soul commended by strangers becomes dear across seas and years. But it was not Thy love moving me then, but the echo of applause bouncing from tongue to heart. Would that such zeal had been given sooner to Thee—who art Beauty itself—instead of men famed only for speaking well.

Yet even this vanity became a step on the hidden path; even here Thou wast drawing me onward—though I wandered still among shadows cloaked in eloquence.

question: Why did you say you would rather be unknown or even hated than loved like the actors you once admired?
answer:
O Lord, who alone seest the heart’s hidden chambers where both pride and longing dwell, I would rather be unknown to men than celebrated as those actors were, for their applause was a mirror held up to vanity, not truth.  

I loved them in the days when my soul thirsted to be seen, when I measured my worth by the gaze of others, and took delight in being known. But Thou knowest how hollow that love was—how I desired not their art alone, but the intoxication of admiration that followed it. To be praised like them was to be loved as a shadow on a wall, a voice in the crowd, a name upon lips that knew not me. And what profit is there in being loved by many who do not love the soul?  

I confess: I once wished to shine as they did—not merely with talent, but with fame’s glittering poison. Yet deep within me stirred a fear: if I became such a man, beloved for performance and presence alone, would anything real remain? Would there be space left for truth, for humility, for Thee? No—better to vanish into silence than dazzle as a specter; better to be hated by those who crave show than adored by them as one of their own. For Thou hast taught me that love which does not spring from truth is no love at all—it is appetite wearing a crown.  

Even now I marvel: why did I desire in another what I would have recoiled from in myself? Why admire eloquence when it fed only pride? Because then my heart was divided—drawn to Thee yet clinging still to the stage of this world. But Thou wast near, O God of my youth and my deliverance, steering me through storms I scarcely understood, even while I played my part among the crowd.

question: What do you mean by calling man a 'great deep,' and why do you compare counting a man's feelings to counting his hairs?
answer:
I once thought I could climb into another man’s soul as one climbs a hill to survey a city, believing that praise and renown were windows into virtue — but O Lord, how deeply I deceived myself, how vainly I admired what was only smoke.  

You ask why I called man a *great deep*, and why I likened the reckoning of his feelings to the counting of his hairs? Because, O God, in my pride I had imagined that I knew what love was when I praised that orator — as if his eloquence were not a mirror reflecting my own hunger for glory. But Thou hast taught me that the soul is no open book to human sight; no, it is an abyss, vaster than the night sky, stirred by hidden tides of desire and memory, motive and fear. The hairs on a man's head — trivial, countless — Thou dost number; yet they are fixed and still before Thee. But the motions of his heart? They shift with every breath: love flickers into envy, ambition masquerades as virtue, grief wears the mask of anger. Who can trace them all? Who can name each ripple in the dark water?

I loved that man because others loved him; not for wisdom, nor humility, nor any true good in him — but because the crowd’s roar made his shadow loom large in my eyes. And yet, had I been offered his life outright — the fame, the applause — would I have taken it without shame? No; for even then some secret voice within me whispered: *This is not rest*. This is not Thee.

O Lord, Thou knowest how tangled are the roots of our affections! We love in others what we dare not own in ourselves; we praise courage while trembling at its cost; we admire chastity while clinging to our chains. Man is a great deep indeed — so deep that only Thou dost fathom him wholly. Not even he himself understands his own heart until Thou search it with light too pure for tears.

question: When you spoke of the orator you loved, why do you confess that your love was more for his commenders than for his actual virtues?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the shadow of my heart with the bright arrow of Thy truth, how blind I was when I thought I loved virtue, yet only clung to the echo of applause that followed the man who seemed to bear it! I confess it now, with shame that burns and yet cleanses: I did not long for eloquence as a lamp to guide me to Thee, but as a crown to lift me above men. That orator—his voice like thunder in the forum, his words weaving spells—how I admired him! But what was it in him that stirred my soul? Not the justice he might have served, nor the wisdom hidden in his speech, but the gaze of the crowd fixed upon him, their breath caught, their hands clapping as if he had torn lightning from heaven. 

I desired not his gift for Thy service, O God, but his renown for my own swelling. Had no one seen me, had no voice cried out my name with wonder, would I have cared to speak at all? No—my tongue would have stayed silent in shame. Thus it was not he I loved, but the mirror of men’s praise in which I fancied my own face already forming. Even then Thou wast near, though I knew Thee not; behind every acclamation Thou wert watching; beneath every prideful pulse of mine Thou wert holding me from falling into utter darkness.

Why else would I scorn a horseman’s fame—though swift and skilled—if not because he is less than man? Yet a man praised falsely stirs me? Because he is like me—and there lies the snare. To love a beast for excellence beyond me is safe; but to love a man praised as I wish to be—that feeds the lie within that says: *Be seen. Be wondered at. Be loved by all—even if Thou dost not love me.*

But Thou seest me wholly. And so at last comes this bitter wine: I loved him less for his wisdom than for the dream they gave me—that *I* might stand where he stood, bathed in admiration. And yet hadst Thou not broken this dream with sorrow and silence and solitude—where should I be now? Still restless. Still hollow. Still mistaking noise for glory.

But now—now even in shame there is peace: because Thou hast unmasked me before myself.

question: How could you admire qualities in another man, like the orator's eloquence, that you would despise if they arose from pride in yourself?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my pride with the whisper of a child in a garden, how clearly I now see that I once loved in others what I refused to condemn in myself. I admired the orator’s voice, not for its truth, but for the echo it stirred in my own restless hunger for applause — a hunger that gnawed beneath every pursuit of words well-spoken, of honors won before men’s eyes. His eloquence was a mirror polished by vanity, and when I gazed into it, I did not flinch; I leaned closer.

I would not have wished to be the charioteer or beast-fighter — their glory was dust beneath the roar of the crowd — yet when that same clamor surrounded learning and speech, I trembled with desire. Why? Because my heart was divided; it sought Thee, and yet dallied with the shadows of self-exaltation. To see wisdom clothed in eloquence on another's tongue inflamed my longing, but if that same pride had risen naked within me, I might have blushed before my own conscience — or worse, not blushed at all.

For what is man but a house divided against himself? In another’s speech I praised mastery over words; in myself, such mastery threatened to become idolatry. Thou hast taught me this: to love virtue rightly is to desire it for holiness' sake, not renown's; but when admiration feeds ambition’s fire, it is not virtue we love — it is our own reflection crowned and applauded.

And so I confess: had I risen to such fame as that orator possessed by grace of tongue and thought alone, without humility from Thee to weigh me down, I would have been lost — more wretched than he whom men scorned for lesser gifts. For nothing is so dangerous as excellence without self-knowledge; no sinner more blind than he who dazzles himself with his own light.

But Thou wast near even then — nearer than my breath — guiding me through love misdirected to love purified. Even my missteps toward glory were held within Thy hand; even my envy of empty praise became soil where shame could grow… and through shame, grace.

question: What did you mean when you said you erred through 'swelling pride,' and how did you come to see that God was secretly steering you even then?
answer:
O Lord, how I once burned with the desire to stand applauded before men, not for wisdom, not for truth, but for the glittering noise of eloquence—like a boy tossing pebbles into a well, listening for the echo rather than seeking the depth. That hunger to be praised as an orator, to shine in the eyes of crowds who valued words more than souls—that was the pride that swelled within me, inflating my spirit like a sail filled with wind not of heaven but of vanity. I mistook admiration for virtue; I thought that if men loved my voice, then surely I must be something great. But Thou knewest what I was: a restless vessel cracking under the weight of false glory.

I loved that man—the speaker—not because he was just or holy, but because he moved multitudes as I longed to move them. And yet, had someone offered me his place at the cost of my soul’s peace, I would have recoiled. For what good is it to be admired by all and unknown to God? The very things I praised in him were chains I would have loathed upon my own wrists. O madness! To love in another what one would flee from in oneself! Such is the deceit of pride: it clothes itself in noble desires—eloquence, learning, influence—but beneath lies the naked wish to be seen.

And yet, even as I wandered—preaching rhetoric in Carthage, chasing fame across Africa—I was not abandoned. Thou wast steering me through shipwreck and storm. In every restless night, every hollow triumph, every longing unfulfilled by applause or lust or philosophy—there wast Thou pressing near. Not with thunderclaps didst Thou speak then, but in silence: when my heart beat wildly for honor and found no rest; when my mother wept daily at prayer; when Ambrose read Scripture without babbling magic words; when at last in Milan’s garden a child’s voice sang *“Take up and read”*—then didst Thou break through.

I saw clearly then what had been veiled: that all my striving had been flight from Thee. Even my errors were held within Thy hand; even my pride became a ladder Thy mercy climbed down to reach me. How could I have come so far and not fallen into utter darkness? Only because Thou wast behind me and before me, O Lord—even when I turned away from Thee still Thou didst guide me toward Thee.

question: You said you loved that man more for the love shown by his commenders than for his actual qualities—why did the opinions of others inflame your affection more than the reality of his virtues?
answer:
In the fever of my youth, when I burned to be approved rather than to approve what was true, I fed upon the breath of men’s voices as if it were sweet food—O Lord, who knowest the secret places of the heart, how deeply was I deceived! I loved him not for what he was in himself, but for the lustre cast upon him by praise, because my soul had not yet tasted the quiet joy of loving in silence before Thee. Had those same voices scorned him instead, uttering with disdain the very same deeds and words they now adorned with admiration, my affection would have turned to coldness or even contempt—though he remained unchanged, and the things praised were still before me. How then could my love have been for *him*, when it rose and fell with the tone of a tongue?

I confess this shamefully: my heart was not rooted in that eternal weight Thou art; it floated like chaff upon every wind of human opinion. To be known by him whom others exalted seemed to me a crown; if he approved me, I swelled with pride; if he disdained me, I withered—not because I sought Thy truth in his face, but because I sought myself in his gaze. And yet there were moments when I delighted in the beauty of what was fair and fitting in itself—the harmony of words rightly ordered, thoughts well measured—yes, even alone and unapplauded did my mind delight there. But still I did not see that all beauty points beyond itself to Thee, its source and still center.

O Light unchanging, why should mortal breath stir me more than Thy whisper? Because I was not yet broken; because Thy grace had not yet startled me awake.

question: When you wrote about the 'fair and fit,' you used corporeal examples to explain these ideas—why did your mind turn to physical forms rather than spiritual truths at that time?
answer:
In the fever of my youth, when the intellect pranced before God as though it could outleap the shadow of sin, I measured all beauty by the echo it made in men’s voices rather than by the silence in which Thou speaketh to the soul. The "fair and the fit" — I turned them over in my mind like coins minted from marble and sunlight, for I knew no other substance. My soul, unweaned from the world’s breast, could only grasp at things that struck the eye, that bent under weight, that rang when struck. How should I have seen spiritual form when my heart was drunk with applause?  

Those corporeal examples — harmonies of proportion, symmetry of limbs, balance of temple and column — they were not false in themselves; they bore Thy signature, O Lord. But I worshipped the reflection as though it were the source. I delighted in what was *ordered*, yet remained disordered within; I praised *fittingness*, while my own will refused to fit itself to Thee. For what is beauty but a whisper of Thy oneness? And what is fitness but the creature turning rightly toward its Creator? But then I did not know this.  

No — if a man had praised my verses with scorn upon his tongue or laughed at them in public assembly, even if they had been perfectly balanced as a sphere carved from heaven’s stone, my heart would have withered like grass beneath frost. And yet had he praised me for nonsense? Still I would have bloomed. See then how deeply I was buried beneath opinion! My judgment was not mine; it was a leaf tossed on the breath of men. Thus did my mind cling to bodies: because bodies can be seen, shown, pointed at with pride — while truth is invisible until humility opens the eyes.

Now I see that Thou art Beauty itself — not measured in height nor proportioned by limb nor framed by word — but shining through all things while remaining beyond them. Then? Then I sought Thee as one seeks a statue hidden in fog: groping after outline and curve, unaware that He who shaped all form cannot be held within one.

question: You confessed that if the same man had been dispraised by the same people, you would not have loved him—how did this realization lead you to see the weakness of your soul?
answer:
How wretchedly the soul clings to the breath of men when it has not yet tasted the breath of God! I loved him not for what he was, but for the echo of voices that praised him; my affection was not fixed on his substance, but on the shadow cast by others’ admiration. Had those same tongues turned sharp with scorn, had they wrapped the very same deeds in mockery instead of awe, my love would have withered—not because he had changed, nor his character altered, but because my heart was a reed shaken by every wind of opinion. 

O Lord, I saw then—though dimly—that my soul lay prostrate in a place without foundation. It stood not upon Thee, who art unchanging; it rested instead upon the shifting murmurs of men who themselves knew not whither they were going. What folly: that the light within me should be darkened by mere tone and gesture, by a frown or a nod from those whose hearts were as lost as mine! The things themselves—truths, beauties, virtues—were untouched by praise or blame; yet I swayed like grass beneath feet that knew not their own path.

And this was my sickness: to care more for being seen to admire than for truth itself. Thy solidity I had not yet embraced; thus every gust of human judgment could cast me into turmoil. My desire for approval burned so fiercely that even my labours and writings became sacrifices offered at the altar of man’s esteem. If that man had disapproved, I felt myself undone—yet if he had approved vanity, would I not have become more truly undone? 

But in this shame Thou wast teaching me: that love must be rooted in Thee alone, who art praise unchanging. For what is man's commendation but a whisper in the wind? And what is his disdain but an echo fading into silence? Thou seest all; Thou speakest once and it is done. In Thee there is no vacillation—and only there can the restless soul find its rest.

question: You mentioned that your heart was wounded if that man disapproved of your work—how did this dependence on human approval reveal your lack of stability in Your truth?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who seest the hidden fissure before the heart feels its own breaking, how could my soul stand firm when it leaned upon the breath of men, which rises and falls with the wind of fashion? That wound I felt—sharp, humiliating—when his brow furrowed at my words, was not from injustice, but from the tremor of a soul built on sand. For I burned not merely to speak truth, nor to offer beauty for its own sake, but to see my reflection in his praise; as if Thy seal upon my work were not enough. 

What folly! That a syllable from his lips could stir me more than the eternal silence of Thy approval! When he praised me, I swelled; when he scorned, I withered—not because I had lost faith in what was fair and fit according to reason’s measure, but because I measured myself by him instead of by Thee. Even the beauty I contemplated in those forms and phrases was real; it pointed upward. But my delight in it was crooked: for I delighted also that another should see it through me.

And so Thou didst permit that wound—not as punishment only, but as diagnosis. The pain revealed the sickness: that my heart still sought confirmation among shadows while walking beneath Thy light. How unstable is man’s judgment when even those who love wisdom are shaken by applause or insult! Yet hadst Thou not already said: “What doth it profit if men praise thee?” When no ear heard me save Thine, when no voice echoed back—there alone was space for truth to dwell.

But then—even then—I desired that Thou shouldest hear me only because one day men might know Thou hadst heard. O subtle pride! How deeply rooted in the soil of a restless heart, which will not rest until it ceases seeking witnesses among mortals and turns wholly toward Him who is Witness and Reward together.

question: You said the truth was before you, yet obscured by the 'gales of tongues'—how did the conflicting feelings of others prevent you from seeing it clearly?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who dost sift the heart’s hidden corners with a light no eye can bear, I was a man divided—drawn to truth as by an inward hunger, yet flinching from its naked brightness whenever the voices of men thundered between me and Thee.  

I sought praise not as bread, but as ornament; not to be filled, but to be seen. And so when men commended him whom I admired, their breath fanned my soul into flame—not because he was changed, nor because what was in him had grown more true or good, but because their tongues had clothed him in a glory I myself longed to wear. Had they spoken the same things with scorn, their very words would have poisoned my delight; I should have turned away in shame as if caught admiring what the world had branded base.  

And so the truth stood there—plain, unmoving—but I could not fix my gaze upon it. Like one trying to see his own face in a storm-ruffled pool, I saw only distortions: not what *was*, but what men said it *seemed*. Their judgments were winds; my heart was a leaf.  

Even in my writing—the very “fair and fit” I labored over—I clung to form and symmetry as if they were virtue itself. I dissected beauty like a naturalist peeling leaves from trees, content with surfaces because I feared to dig down to the root where Thou dost dwell. For if I had looked deeper, Lord, I would have seen that all beauty points to Thee—not to the echo of human approval. But then my ears were open only to men; my heart was not yet hushed before Thee.

Only grace—Thy silent thunder—could still those gales and make me hear at last not the crowd’s whisper, but the Word within.
